caption:
Fundus photography of the left eye The fovea centralis looks dark red (cherry-red spot; green overlay) due to the transparency of this part of the retina lacking nerve fibres, causing the well-vascularized choroid to shine through. The surrounding retina is pale and opacified, indicating ischemic edema. Narrow retinal arterioles with box-carring (examples indicated by arrowheads) are also visible.
These findings are typical of acute central retinal artery occlusion.

Ultrasound liver of a patient with hepatocellular carcinoma The surface of the liver is irregular (green line) as a result of cirrhosis. A solitary liver lesion demonstrates an isoechoic center (green hatched overlay) and hypoechoic periphery (green overlay). Such an appearance, which has been termed the “halo sign” (also “target sign” or “bull's eye sign”), may indicate a malignant liver lesion. Hepatocellular carcinoma has variable presentations on ultrasound.

The middle finger shows numerous superficial, round depressions (nail pits; examples indicated by arrowheads) caused by deficient keratinization. Both the ring and the middle finger show distal separation of the nail plate (onycholysis; green overlay). The index finger appears mostly normal. Nail pitting and onycholysis are typical features of psoriasis with nail involvement.

CT abdomen (with contrast; axial plane) Multiple hypodense lesions in the liver demonstrate subtle peripheral enhancement (examples indicated by blue overlay). Also shown are bilateral pleural effusions (green overlay), abdominal ascites (yellow overlay), and an atherosclerotic plaque (red overlay) in the aorta.

Photograph of a dissected heart specimen opened to show the endocardial aspect of left atrium and ventricle The mitral valve is thickened (indicated by arrow) and shows numerous yellow vegetations on the atrial (superior) aspect of the valve (examples indicated by arrowheads). This gross appearance is typical of infective endocarditis of the mitral valve.

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Multiple granulomas (examples indicated by green overlays) showing no central necrosis, known as noncaseating granulomas are visible. Noncaseating granulomas on lung biopsy are a typical finding in pulmonary sarcoidosis.

Photomicrograph of the gastric antrum (H&E stain) There is marked hyperplasia of G cells (green overlay) in the middle of the gastric glands. The G cells have the characteristic “fried egg” appearance. Use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) is a common cause of G-cell hyperplasia.

Photomicrograph of a kidney biopsy sample (PAS stain; very high magnification) The glomerulus shows a hypercellular, crescent-shaped zone (green overlay) with an inflammatory infiltrate (examples of macrophages indicated by arrowheads). Glomerular crescent formation is a characteristic histological finding in RPGN.
Multiple hypopigmented macules (after UV exposure) are visible on this patient's back. This appearance is suggestive of pityriasis versicolor alba.

MRI brain (T1-weighted; axial plane) There is marked dilatation of the lateral ventricles (green overlay) with mild sulcal enlargement (red overlay). The findings are suggestive of normal pressure hydrocephalus as opposed to age-related or neurodegenerative cerebral atrophy. Periventricular hypointensities (examples indicated by white arrowheads) are also seen and may reflect transependymal edema due to elevated cerebrospinal fluid pressure.

X-ray right knee (AP view) An intraarticular body (loose body, joint mouse; arrow) is visible within the medial compartment of the knee joint. Intraarticular bodies are comprised of cartilage or cartilage and bone and may be the result of injury or degenerative disease. In the case of osteochondritis dissecans, a donor site (not shown here) is sometimes seen along the convex articular surface of a femoral condyle following the separation of an osteochondral fragment.
Basic structure of a class G immunoglobulin (IgG) The arrow indicates the Fc region of the molecule.

CT chest (with contrast; axial plane; mediastinal window) of a patient with Marfan syndrome A large aneurysm of the ascending thoracic aorta (AA) is seen. The diameter of the descending aorta (DA) is normal.

Photomicrograph of a liver biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Most of the hepatocytes have a pale, homogeneous cytoplasm (ground glass hepatocytes; examples marked by blue overlay). Ground glass hepatocytes are often found in chronic hepatitis B infection.

Visual field test (perimetry) There is a bilateral loss of right lateral visual fields (black areas) with sparing of the macula (hatched overlay). Bilateral impairment of a unilateral visual field in each eye is known as homonymous hemianopia.

Photomicrograph of an urethral smear specimen (Gram stain with fuchsin counterstain; high magnification) The smear shows an abundance of neutrophils recognizable by their typical segmented nuclei and accumulations of diplococci, in the intracellular (within neutrophils; examples indicated by white arrowheads in magnification) and extracellular space (examples indicated by black arrowheads). This microscopic finding is characteristic of acute gonorrhea caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Diagnosis must be confirmed by cultural isolation and identification of the pathogens.
Endoscopic image of the esophagus Circumferential mucosal lesions (rings/corrugations; examples indicated by blue overlay) are visible. Yellow lines: longitudinal furrows; Dashed encirclement: Z-line

Fundus photography of the right eye of a 76-year-old patient Flame-shaped retinal hemorrhage is visible in all four quadrants (example indicated by green outline). The tortuous veins (green arrows) are markedly dilated and there is papilledema with loss of sharp demarcation of the optic disc (green overlay). These findings are typical for a central retinal vein occlusion.

Hyperpigmented skin and hyperkeratosis are visible in this patient's left axilla. An erythematous patch is visible in between the hyperpigmented areas. These findings are suggestive of acanthosis nigricans.

Photomicrograph of a bone biopsy specimen (H&E stain) Osteoid (appearing as a homogeneous, partially strand-like, eosinophilic substance) interspersed with tightly packed spindle cells and some osteoclast-like, multinucleated giant cells (examples indicated by green overlay). The tissue shows typical hallmarks of malignancy, e.g., high mitotic index, irregular sizes and shapes of cells and nuclear membranes (examples indicated by arrowheads), and the presence of osteoclast-like giant cells. This is the typical histological appearance of osteosarcoma.
Sites of action in the nephron of different classes of diuretics.

CT of the thorax (axial view, with IV contrast) A large mass (green overlay) is visible in the posterior mediastinum. The most common posterior mediastinal mass is a neurogenic tumor (biopsy is necessary for confirmation).
Multiple papules and erythema are visible on the forearm at the site of contact with urushiol. This is the typical appearance of contact dermatitis.

X-ray chest (PA view) of patient with history of rib fracture from a fall 10 days prior and left-sided chest pain accompanied by new dyspnea and orthopnea The margins of the descending thoracic aorta (dashed line) and left hemidiaphragm are obscured by extensive air space disease (green overlay) and pleural effusion; a meniscus (red line) helps confirm the presence of the effusion. In view of the trauma history, the likely causes are pulmonary contusion and hemothorax. The slightly high position of the gastric bubble (GB) also suggests the presence of left lower lobe atelectasis. The right lung and cardiac silhouette are normal. Circle: fracture of the anterior left seventh rib

Photomicrograph of a kidney biopsy specimen (H&E stain; approx. 400x) Multiple intra-arterial spindle-shaped vacuoles ('cholesterol clefts'; examples: C) are surrounded by multinucleated giant-cells (GC) and epithelioid cells (E). The arterial lumen has largely been filled by fibrotic tissue (green overlay). The tunica media has partially atrophied (white overlay), while the tunica adventitia (Adv) appears normal. These findings are highly suggestive of renal cholesterol embolism.

Fingers (A) and thumb (B) of the right hand of a 22-year-old female patient presenting with weakness, weight loss, and reduced appetite The nails are centrally concave and bend upwards distally, resulting in a characteristic spoon shape. The most common cause of koilonychia is iron deficiency anemia, as was the case here.

MRI head (T2-weighted; axial plane) of a patient with a 3-day history of headache, nausea, forgetfulness, low-grade fever, and seizures
Cortical and subcortical T2 hyperintensity (green overlay) is demonstrated within the left medial temporal lobe associated with mild gyral expansion. The differential diagnosis included post-ictal changes and viral encephalitides of different origins, with neoplasm and other causes being less likely. PCR of the CSF confirmed the diagnosis of herpes simplex encephalitis.
T: temporal lobe; P: pons; C: cerebellum

X-ray left ankle (mortise view) A lucent fracture line (green line) through the medial malleolus traverses both the epiphysis and growth plate. This appearance is characteristic of a Salter-Harris III fracture. Growth plate involvement is often better seen on CT. E: epiphysis; F: fibula; GP: growth plate; T: tibia
While the right eye fixates straight, the left eye deviates upwards (hypertropia). This may be due to congenital superior oblique palsy or trochlear nerve palsy.

Glycogenesis:
(A) Glucose (Glc) and ATP is phosphorylated into glucose 6-phosphate (Glc-6-P) and ADP. Glc-6-P can be converted into glucose 1-phosphate (Glc-1-P). Glc-1-P and uridine triphosphate (UTE) is then converted to uridine diphosphate (UDP) glucose and pyrophosphate (PPi)
(B) Glycogen chains are elongated by the binding of a glucose residue to the free end of the glycogen chain, releasing UDP.
(C) Branching enzyme transfers chains of glucose residues to a new site, adding a new branch to the glycogen molecule. Glycogenolysis within the cytosol:
(D) Glc-1-P is released via a phosphoric reaction from the glycogen molecule until 4 terminal glucose residues remain. Glu-1-P is then converted into Glc-6-p and finally hydrolyzed into glucose and inorganic phosphate (Pi), see (A).
(E) 3 out of the 4 remaining glucose residues from a branch are transferred to a nearby branch, the remaining glucose residue is cleaved via hydrolysis releasing free glucose. Glycogenolysis within lysosomes:
(F) Glucose residues are cleaved via hydrolysis releasing free glucose.
Photomicrograph of bacteria in blood culture (Kinyoun stain) Clusters of branching, filamentous rods are concentrated in the center.

A: left gracile fasciculus 
B: left cuneate fasciculus 
C: left lateral spinothalamic tract
D: left ventral spinothalamic tract
E: right ventral spinothalamic tract
F: right lateral spinothalamic tract
G: right cuneate fasciculus
H: right gracile fasciculus

X-ray ankle (right; AP view) after supination-external rotation injury An oblique fracture of the distal fibula (yellow overlay and red line) is accompanied by an avulsion fracture (red lines) of the medial malleolus (green overlay). The ankle mortise has been disrupted. There is widening of the medial compartment (indicated by white arrow) and lateral displacement of the talus (indicated by a black arrow). A lateral view (not included here) showed a fracture of the posterior malleolus. The findings indicate an unstable trimalleolar fracture that requires operative treatment. If classified based on the location of the fibular fracture (Danis-Weber classification), this would be a Weber type B injury.

A solitary, pigmented, firm circular lesion is visible. Upon pinching of the lesion, the overlaying skin retracts inwards (dimple sign). This appearance is suggestive of a dermatofibroma, which usually manifests as a nodule.
Multiple erythematous, partly excoriated papules and burrows are visible on the back of the hand, the fingers, and the intertriginous area.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Regularly irregular rhythm
- Atrial rate of ∼ 300/min and ventricular rate of ∼ 150/min
- Normal axis (R > S in I and aVF)
- Sawtooth P-wave morphology (flutter/F waves) best seen in II, III, aVF (indicated by green lines) These findings are indicative of atrial flutter with 2:1 AV block.

MRI head (T2-weighted; sagittal plane) of a neonate The brainstem (red overlay) is elongated. The medulla (M), fourth ventricle (yellow overlay), and cerebellum (green overlay) are displaced caudally through the foramen magnum (white dashed line).

CT scan of the thorax (axial view) An intimal flap (dashed line) resulting in a double lumen is seen in the descending aorta. The smaller lumen (true lumen; red overlay) shows high contrast density. The larger lumen (false lumen; green overlay) shows low-contrast density due to delayed enhancement. This appearance is typical of an aortic dissection. Arrows: atelectasis; AA: ascending aorta; PA: pulmonary artery
A sharply demarcated darkly pigmented papule with a greasy, stuck-on appearance is visible. This is the typical appearance of seborrheic keratosis.

Rhythm strip ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Atrial rate ∼100/min
– Ventricular rate ~40/min (escape rhythm)
– RR intervals and PP intervals are regular.
– Atrioventricular (AV) dissociation: P waves (red overlay) and QRS complexes (green overlay) have regular but unrelated rhythms because there is no conduction between atria and ventricles, resulting in some P waves being completely or partially buried in QRS complexes (6th P wave) or T waves (4th and 9th P waves). AV dissociation with bradycardia is characteristic of third-degree AV block (complete heart block).

Cranial MRI (with contrast, axial view) An oval lesion can be seen in the left temporal lobe, with ring-like contrast enhancement (green ring) and central necrosis (green overlay). The perifocal edema (white overlay) that is visible corresponds to the lesion. Differential diagnoses include an abscess, glioblastoma, and metastasis. Diagnosis: abscess following advanced otitis media

Photomicrograph of a stool sample (wet mount; iodine stain) An oval-shaped cyst with a prominent, double-layered wall is visible in the center of the image. The cytoplasm contains four nuclei, which are identifiable as spherical structures. It also contains an axostyle, a fibrillary structure from which the flagella of the trophozoite arises. This is the typical appearance of a Giardia lamblia cyst, which is excreted in feces and responsible for the transmission of giardiasis through the ingestion of contaminated food or water.
A diffuse rash is seen in various stages of lesion evolution (starry-sky appearance), with mainly ruptured vesicles and crusted erosions seen here. These findings are typical for chickenpox.

Microscopic image of the thymus (H&E stain, 25x magnification) The thymus retains its characteristic two-layered structure until puberty. The lymphocyte-rich cortex (dark purple) is clearly distinguishable from medulla with its lower density of lymphocytes and presence of thymic corpuscles (red). Click on the microscope icon to view the entire specimen through a virtual microscope.

Prominent blood vessels are visible on the sclera and conjunctiva (mixed injection; examples indicated by arrowheads). A dense corneal infiltration (white opacification; green overlay) is visible at the center of the cornea. The peripheral cornea shows slight opacification indicating corneal decompensation (blurred details of the iris). An accumulation of white-yellow exudate (hypopyon; blue overlay) consisting of inflammatory cells and/or bacteria/fungi within the anterior chamber can be seen completely covering the inferior aspect of the iris. These findings are consistent with infective keratitis, associated here with contact lens use.
A circular erythematous rash with a central clearing (“bulls-eye” rash) is visible on the upper back. Erythema migrans is typically seen in the early localized stage of Lyme disease.

Train-of-four stimulations are done by delivering four successive supramaximal stimuli (at 500 ms intervals) to a peripheral nerve. Those groups of four stimulations are repeatedly delivered to evaluate the resulting pattern of muscular response. Normally, the resulting muscular responses (as measured via electromyography) should be equally strong (both within groups of four consecutive responses and over a succession of consecutive groups of such responses). If nondepolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., rocuronium) are administered, the amplitude of muscular responses decreases within a group of four consecutive responses (that the amplitude of the fourth response is significantly lower than that of the first one of the same group). This phenomenon is also referred to as “fade.” The effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., succinylcholine) are subdivided into two phases: In phase I, the drug binds to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, inducing a single, persistent depolarization. Electromyographically, each of the individual responses in a group of four will be decreased by the same percentage, and the overall muscular responsiveness between consecutive groups of stimuli steadily decreases over time. If depolarizing neuromuscular agents are administered over a prolonged amount of time or a high dose is given, phase II may occur. In this phase, there is a fade in the muscular response, resembling the phenomenon seen in nondepolarizing agents. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., neostigmine) potentiate the effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs during phase I.

Multiple hyperpigmented brown macules are visible on this older patient's hand. This is the characteristic appearance and location of solar lentigo, which results from exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Fragmented erythrocytes, which are convex on one side (examples indicated by blue lines) and concave on the other (examples indicated by yellow lines), can be seen among the normal erythrocytes. These fragmented erythrocytes are referred to as schistocytes. Schistocytes are a diagnostic finding in patients with microangiopathic hemolytic anemias and those with mechanical damage to erythrocytes (e.g., from artificial heart valves).

X-ray right forearm and wrist (PA view) of a young child Fall on the outstretched hand caused axial loading of the radius and ulna along their long axes. The resultant trabecular compression (yellow overlay) produced torus (buckle; green overlay) fractures of each metaphysis. Ossification centers in the wrist and hand have not yet fully developed in this child. The normal cartilaginous growth plate (physis; red overlay) of the radius is visible as a lucent region.

MRI brain (T1-weighted; axial plane) There is marked dilatation of the lateral ventricles (green overlay) with mild sulcal enlargement (red overlay). The findings are suggestive of normal pressure hydrocephalus as opposed to age-related or neurodegenerative cerebral atrophy. Periventricular hypointensities (examples indicated by white arrowheads) are also seen and may reflect transependymal edema due to elevated cerebrospinal fluid pressure.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Irregularly irregular rhythm (examples of the irregular RR intervals indicated by red arrows)
– Average ventricular rate approx. 132/min (22 QRS complexes can be seen on the 10-second rhythm strip)
– Normal axis
– Narrow QRS complexes
– No discernable P waves
– Coarse fibrillatory waves (F waves) are visible (best seen in aVR, aVF, rhythm strip II; examples indicated by arrowheads).

CT abdomen (with IV contrast; axial plane) Sharply demarcated gas bubbles in a linear, branching pattern can be seen in the biliary tract (green overlay). This is the characteristic appearance of pneumobilia. A: Abdominal aorta, Li: Liver, Lu: Lungs, Sp: Spleen, St: Stomach, VC: Inferior vena cava

Cranial MRI (T2-weighted, axial view) The butterfly-shaped lateral ventricles are symmetrically dilated and filled with CSF (green overlay). The cerebral gyri appear atrophic (example indicated by white asterisk) and the sulci are deep and prominent (example indicated by black arrow). These features are characteristic of hydrocephalus ex vacuo, a condition in which the ventricles dilate due to brain parenchymal atrophy and CSF fills the void.

A solitary ulcer, approx. 2 cm in diameter, is visible on the lower lip. The inferior aspect of the ulcer appears hyperkeratotic. This finding is highly suggestive of squamous cell carcinoma. Oral squamous cell carcinoma most commonly affects the lower lip because of its direct exposure to sunlight.

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy specimen (H&E stain, high magnification) Multiple nests of small, round, monomorphic cells can be seen throughout the tissue (hatched green overlay). Some of these cells are arranged in rosette formations (green overlay). The chromatin is finely grained in the monomorphic cells (salt-and-pepper chromatin). Hyperemic capillaries between the cell nests indicate rich vascularization (examples indicated by green arrowheads). These findings are consistent with a carcinoid tumor.
There is erythema, telangiectasia, papules, and pustules localized to the nose and cheeks. These are characteristic features of rosacea.

An erythematous nodule with a central indentation that developed in the course of 7 weeks. These findings are consistent with keratoacanthoma, which typically manifests with a central hyperkeratotic plug, leaving an indentation after removal (as seen here).
Curve A is the normal curve of oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation, whereas curve B shows a right shift (decreased affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen).

X-ray chest (AP view) of a patient with a recent history of a motor vehicle crash A lobulated convexity projects along the left superior mediastinum (green overlay and arrows) in the region of the aortic knob. The differential for the mediastinal widening includes a range of mediastinal soft tissue masses; however, traumatic mediastinal injury (particularly involving the thoracic aorta) is the primary concern in view of the trauma setting. CT angiography is the appropriate next imaging study in a hemodynamically stable patient. Green dashed line: normal margin of the aorta

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm), with a baseline of approx. 150 bpm (green dashed line overlay). The FHR lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and shows minimal variability (< 6 bpm; normal range 6–25 bpm). The lower blue tracing shows maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg). The decelerations begin after the peaks of the uterine contractions (red dashed line overlay). They are gradual, with ≥ 30 s from onset to nadir (red arrow overlay). These findings are characteristic of late decelerations.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Erythrocytes with a teardrop shape can be seen throughout the image (red overlay). The majority of the erythrocytes appear round and normal. Teardrop-shaped erythrocytes, known as dacrocytes, are found in conditions that involve extramedullary hematopoiesis (e.g., myelofibrosis, thalassemia, splenomegaly).

X-ray soft tissue neck (lateral view) The epiglottis is swollen and rounded in appearance (thumb sign; highlighted by green overlay and black arrow) as a result of inflammation and edema. The normal air space between the tongue base and epiglottis has been almost entirely obliterated and is seen only as a shallow V-shaped space (vallecula sign; highlighted by red line). The thumb and vallecula signs are useful in the radiographic evaluation of epiglottitis.
Forearms of a child A maculopapular, erythematous rash with a lace-like pattern is visible on both forearms.

Endoscopic view of the colon The mucosa is erythematous and edematous indicating active inflammation. Mucosal granularity, loss of superficial vascular markings, and ulcerations (exemplarily highlighted by green overlay) are also present. These findings are characteristic of acute ulcerative colitis.

Fundus photography of the right eye There is detachment of the neurosensory retina (blue overlay) from the retinal pigment epithelium reaching from temporal (left side of image) to central involving the fovea centralis, creating an opaque, corrugated appearance of the retina compared to the intact medial side. A retinal tear was identified superotemporally at the 11-o'clock position (not shown here), classifying this as a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment. Blue circle: Optic disc
M: Macula

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Narrow-complex tachycardia with a ventricular rate of approx. 150/min
- Likely atrial fibrillation (irregular RR intervals, no definitive P waves)
- Normal cardiac axis (R > S in both I and aVF)
- QRS voltage is low, and consecutive R waves alternate in height (electrical alternans, red lines) The combination of tachycardia, low QRS voltage, and electrical alternans is highly suggestive of a large pericardial effusion.

Photomicrograph of a breast tissue biopsy specimen (H&E stain; 20x magnification) Necrotic adipocytes (example indicated by yellow overlay) are identifiable as adipocytes with indistinct, irregular borders and absent nuclei. Unstained, lipid-filled cystic spaces are visible in the center of the image (green overlay). Inflammatory infiltrates can be seen at the bottom-right (example indicated by black arrowhead) and on the left side of the image. These findings are characteristic of fat necrosis of the breast. At a higher magnification, cholesterol clefts, foam cells, and multinucleated giant cells would also be identifiable.
There are patches of hair of varying length. The hair density is normal (including new hair growth).

Ultrasound abdomen (liver; left: color Doppler, right: contrast-enhanced ultrasound) A multiseptated hepatic lesion with an anechoic fluid center (left image) shows no vascular enhancement on color Doppler. The necrotic center also does not enhance on contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS; right image), although enhancement of the septae and surrounding parenchyma on CEUS increases the conspicuity of the lesion.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with lung abscess A large thick-walled (green overlay) cavity projecting over the perihilar region of the right lung contains an air-fluid level (indicated by red line). The differential for this appearance includes abscess (bacterial, mycobacterial, fungal), cavitary noninfectious granuloma, and cavitary malignant lesion.

Multiple yellowish, smooth nodules are visible on the dorsum of both hands.
This appearance is suggestive of xanthoma tendinosum.
A round, ulcerative lesion with well-defined margins and a central induration is visible on the right labium majorum. This finding is consistent with primary syphilitic chancre.

Multiple erythematous maculonodular lesions of varying shapes and sizes (green overlay) distributed on both pretibial surfaces. Those findings, especially when seen on the anterior leg, are typical of erythema nodosum.
The erythematous appearance indicates that those lesions have likely developed within the last 7 days (afterwards, erythema nodosum lesions usually turn bluish and then yellow-brownish).

CT lumbar spine (without contrast; axial plane; soft tissue window) The left psoas muscle is enlarged by a hypodense mass (green overlay). Involvement also extends to the level of the adjacent neural foramen and posteriorly to obliterate fat planes of the paraspinal musculature. Findings represent a psoas abscess with paraspinal extension.

X-ray chest (A: PA view; B: lateral view) of a patient with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia Bilateral airspace and interstitial opacities are predominant in the perihilar regions of both lungs, in the left greater than the right (examples indicated by green overlay). The heart size is normal and there are no pleural effusions.

A circular pattern of skin darkening, scaling, and peeling is visible around the neck, an area of skin that is regularly exposed to sunlight. These findings suggest pellagra, which may occur anywhere on the body but especially affects hands, arms, lower legs, feet, face, and neck.

CT head (with contrast; axial plane) of a patient with neurocysticercosis A rounded peripheral lesion in the right temporoparietal region has a hypodense center (yellow overlay) and thickened enhancing wall (red overlay). There is perilesional low-attenuation edema (green overlay). These findings are typical of the colloidal-vesicular stage of neurocysticercosis.

X-ray abdomen (left lateral decubitus view) Gaseous distention of a closed loop of sigmoid colon (red overlay) has caused apposition of the medial walls (green lines). The resultant cleft-like configuration, in conjunction with the lateral walls of the loop, produces an appearance reminiscent of a coffee bean (illustration). The coffee bean sign (also called “kidney-bean sign” or “bent-inner-tube sign”) is a classic conventional radiographic finding of sigmoid volvulus. Dashed line: air-fluid level
A round, swollen nodule can be seen at the dorsal wrist. This appearance is characteristic of a ganglion cyst.

ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular sinus rhythm with a heart rate of 37/min
– PR interval normal (∼ 160 ms), QRS normal (∼ 110 ms), QT interval normal (∼ 480 ms; QTc = 380 ms)
– Morphologies of P wave, QRS complex, and T wave are physiologic. Note the U waves (occurring after the T waves).
– No signs of ischemia

Photomicrograph of a lung tissue specimen (Mucicarmine stain; medium power magnification) Numerous ovoid yeasts of varying size with thick capsules (stained red to violet), some showing narrow-based budding (examples indicated by arrowheads), can be found within the alveoli. This appearance suggests infection with Cryptococcus neoformans.

X-ray chest (PA view) of an adult male patient The lungs are well expanded. No abnormal parenchymal opacities are seen. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. The pulmonary hila and superior mediastinum are also normal. The lateral costophrenic recesses are sharp, with no evidence of effusion. No concerning skeletal lesions are visible. Green arrowheads: right paratracheal stripe; green overlay: aortopulmonary (aortic-pulmonic) window
White arrowhead: example of a spinous process
White dashed lines: tracheal and bronchial outline
Yellow overlay: examples of vertebral pedicles
Red overlay: azygos vein
LDPA: left descending pulmonary artery (or left interlobar artery)
RDPA: right descending pulmonary artery (or right interlobar artery)

Localization: proximal penile shaft
Description: painless, superficial ulcer with indurated borders
Diagnosis: chancre of primary syphilis

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of a 5-day-old newborn A massively dilated colon (green overlay) is seen proximal to a narrowed distal segment (red overlay). The appearance and location of the narrowing are consistent with short-segment Hirschsprung disease. Yellow overlay: gastric bubble

A: anterior cruciate ligament
B: lateral meniscus
C: fibular collateral ligament
D: posterior cruciate ligament
E: medial meniscus
F: tibial collateral ligament

Photomicrograph of a wet mount of human feces A yellowish-brown, round ovum with a knobby (mammillated) outer layer is visible. This is the typical appearance of an Ascaris lumbricoides egg (the round shape indicates that it is fertilized).

Photomicrograph of a bone marrow smear (Wright-Giemsa stain, high magnification) Promyelocytes with azurophilic granules can be seen as well as a myeloblast containing multiple violet, rod-like intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies, known as Auer rods (examples indicated by yellow lines). Auer rods are typically seen in certain types of acute myeloid leukemia (e.g., APL) and in myelodysplastic syndromes.

Photomicrographs of a substantia nigra tissue sample (A: H&E stain, 500x magnification; B: immunochemical α-synuclein stain, 400x magnification) Image A: An eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusion (Lewy body; blue overlay) is visible inside a neuron. There is a large accumulation of neuromelanin (brown granules; green overlay). Image B: A neurite staining positive for α-synuclein is visible in the center of the image (Lewy neurite; yellow overlay). Lewy bodies and neurites are seen in various neurodegenerative disorders, e.g., Parkinson's disease.

CT chest (with contrast; axial plane; mediastinal window) A homogeneous low-attenuation pleural effusion (green overlay) is seen in the dependent right hemithorax. The upward concave configuration of the anterior margin of the effusion is the result of elastic recoil of the lung. AA: ascending aorta; DA: descending aorta; LA: left atrium; LV: left ventricle; RA: right atrium; RV: right ventricle

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with a history of tetralogy of Fallot The cardiac apex is upturned from right ventricular hypertrophy, and the pulmonary artery segment is small and concave in appearance as a result of pulmonary stenosis. These findings produce a characteristic boot-shaped heart (green overlay; cf. illustration), seen in some patients with tetralogy of Fallot. Pulmonary vascular markings are diminished as a result of reduced blood flow to the lungs. White dashed outline: normal size and shape of the heart

Numerous dark-brown, pigmented macules are visible on the lips and in the perioral region (perioral lentigines). This is a characteristic cutaneous manifestation of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome. Similar spots are typically found on the buccal mucosa, palms, and soles.

Maculopapular, reddish, non-blanching, palpable skin lesions (purpura) are seen on both the distal right arm and leg. On the lower leg, the lesions are partially confluent and present with scaling and erosion.

Photomicrograph of a fungal culture (India ink stain) Numerous spherical yeast cells of different sizes can be seen. The polysaccharide capsules appear as yellowish halos around the cell walls (dark circles). These features are characteristic of Cryptococcus neoformans under light microscopy.

Endoscopic image of the esophagus Circumferential lesions (examples indicated by green lines) and longitudinal furrows (examples indicated by blue lines) are visible along the esophageal mucosa. The pattern created by the repeated circumferential lesions is referred to as “trachealization” because it has a similar appearance to a trachea. These are characteristic endoscopic features of eosinophilic esophagitis.

Pelvic x-ray (AP) Slipped epiphysis with femoral neck displaced in relation to the femoral head (green overlay) and widened joint space on the left side. The right femoral head and epiphyseal plate show no pathology.

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm), with a baseline of approx. 150 bpm (green dashed line overlay). The FHR lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and shows minimal variability (< 6 bpm; normal range 6–25 bpm). The lower blue tracing shows maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg). The decelerations begin after the peaks of the uterine contractions (red dashed line overlay). They are gradual, with ≥ 30 s from onset to nadir (red arrow overlay). These findings are characteristic of late decelerations.
Numerous macules and papules, both isolated and in clusters, are visible on the back. While this finding is typical of bedbug bites, they can appear in a wide variety of cutaneous reactions.

Autopsy specimen of a heart (close-up of the longitudinally opened ventricle) A papillary muscle of an atrioventricular valve is ruptured (white arrow) and almost completely detached from its valve-sided end (green arrow). Papillary muscle rupture is a complication of myocardial infarction and, in this case, most likely due to occlusion of a branch of the right coronary artery (e.g., posterior descending artery).

MRI head (T1-weighted; sagittal plane) The hummingbird sign (illustration; also known as “penguin sign”) refers to the appearance of the brainstem on MRI in the sagittal plane in patients with progressive supranuclear palsy. The atrophic mesencephalon (red overlay) represents the bird's head, with the beak extending towards the optic chiasm. The preserved pons (green overlay) represents the body of the bird. Yellow overlay: medulla oblongata

Photomicrograph of a cervical pap smear (Papanicolaou stain; high magnification) Normal superficial squamous cells (pink cytoplasm) as well as some intermediate squamous cells are visible. In the center of the image is an irregular clump of koilocytes (marked by an arrow). The koilocytes are characterized by perinuclear halos and show slight nuclear enlargement with irregularly distributed chromatin; they may also exhibit binucleation. These are typical features of a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL).

Microscopic image of the splenic red pulp (H&E stain) Macrophages with a lipid-laden cytoplasm (examples: green overlay). Those cells are often referred to as “foam cells” and are a typical finding in Niemann-Pick disease.

Marked erythema and streaky, indurated plaques with numerous oozing vesicles and bullae are visible on the back of the hand. The appearance and, especially, the pattern of the lesion are typical of allergic contact dermatitis.

Photomicrograph showing the layers of the adrenal gland (A) Capsule
(B) Cortex, zona glomerulosa
(C) Cortex, zona fasciculata
(D) Cortex, zona reticularis
(E) Medulla
Click on the microscope icon to view the entire specimen through a virtual microscope.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Irregularly irregular rhythm with a ventricular rate of ∼ 84/min (75–125/min; examples indicated by RR interval-lines)
– P waves cannot be identified (no stable isoelectric baseline; instead, fine fibrillatory waves, i.e., f waves, are seen throughout; examples indicated by green circles)
– Normal heart axis (R > S in both I and aVF)
– QRS complexes are borderline narrow (∼ 80 ms). As the ventricular rate varies, no reliable QTc time can be established These findings are characteristic of atrial fibrillation

A: Glioblastoma (adults)
B: Oligodendroglioma (adults)
C: Pituitary adenoma (adults) and craniopharyngioma (children and adults)
D: Pinealoma (children)
E: Pilocytic astrocytoma (children)
F: Medulloblastoma (children)
G: Ependymoma (children and adults)

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with a history of aneurysmal dilatation of the thoracic aorta The mediastinum is widened, with the descending thoracic aorta (white line) extending further to the left than is normal (normal course estimated by white dashed line). The margin of the ascending aorta also extends to the right to a greater degree than normal (white line). Widening of the mediastinum on a chest radiograph as a result of an abnormal aortic contour can be seen in several settings, among them age-related aortic ectasia, aneurysmal dilatation, and dissection. Further imaging (e.g., computed tomography) may be required. A fine curvilinear structure (azygos fissure) courses inferiorly in the medial right apex, terminating at an opaque, tear-shaped structure, which represents the azygos vein (green overlay). The finding is a normal anatomical variant formed by displacement of the azygos vein during fetal development. An azygos fissure is comprised of two layers of parietal pleura and two layers of visceral pleura. Lung medial to the fissure is called an azygos lobe. AK: aortic knob

Coronal sulcus on an adult penis with three solitary, round, ulcerating lesions. This finding indicates granuloma inguinale, a sexually transmitted bacterial disease caused by Klebsiella granulomatis. Granuloma inguinale is endemic to tropical and subtropical countries.

A vesicular eruption with multiple monomorphic, circular, partially confluent lesions, many of which show central umbilication, is seen on the right shoulder of a patient with atopic dermatitis. This appearance is suggestive of eczema herpeticum.

A large, primary lesion with circular erythema and central clearing on the posterior side of the right upper arm is visible. This clinical picture is typical of erythema migrans (stage-I Lyme disease). Illustration: Bull's eye sign

X-ray elbow (lateral view) of a child with a history of recent trauma A comminuted supracondylar fracture of the distal humerus (H) shows posterior displacement of the distal fragment and growth plate involvement (white arrow). Joint effusion is present, with elevation of the lucent posterior fat pad (white arrowheads). H: humerus; R: radius; U: ulna

X-ray chest (PA view) There is marked opacification (green overlay) of the middle and lower left hemithorax from pleural effusion and passive atelectasis of the adjacent lung. No gastric air bubble is seen projecting over the lower left hemithorax to suggest marked elevation of the left hemidiaphragm from a large amount of atelectasis, but concave upper lateral borders, or menisci (white dashed lines), help confirm the presence of effusion extending to the mid thorax. The margins of the cardiac silhouette and left hemidiaphragm are obscured. Additionally, the cardiac silhouette is shifted slightly to the right (indicated by blue line and arrow) and the left bronchial tree is slightly elevated (indicated by black lines and arrow). In the right hemithorax, a small meniscus from effusion is seen laterally (indicated by red line). Additionally, increased opacification projects through the right hemidiaphragm indicating effusion in the posterior sulcus (red overlay).

CT head (without contrast; axial plane) Abnormal low attenuation zones are seen in the periventricular white matter adjacent to the frontal and temporal horns of the lateral ventricles (green overlay). The cerebral sulci (examples indicated by red overlay) and ventricles are also prominent. These imaging features may be seen in subcortical atherosclerotic encephalopathy (vascular dementia, Binswanger type).

CT abdomen (with contrast; axial plane) The lower poles of the kidneys (green overlay) are fused across the midline by an isthmus of enhancing parenchyma (red overlay). Note the lack of parenchyma at the level of the left renal hilum (indicated by white arrow), which is directed anteriorly as a result of incomplete medial rotation of the kidney along its longitudinal axis. This is a classic appearance of horseshoe kidney. C: colon (with contrast)
The right pinna bears two well-defined, round, red swellings with a smooth surface. The swellings extend well beyond the boundary of the original defects (i.e., piercings in the scapha of the pinna).

Several papules and pustules with surrounding erythema (examples indicated by white arrowheads) are visible around the hair follicles on the anterior thigh. These findings are characteristic of folliculitis.

Photomicrograph of a lung tissue biopsy sample Numerous brown, spindle-shaped bodies (ferruginous bodies), many of which are fragmented, are interspersed throughout the specimen (examples indicated by green overlay). Ferruginous bodies are accumulations of asbestos fibers covered with iron and protein, giving them their characteristic dumbbell shape (cf. illustration). The asbestos fibers themselves are not visible.

Barium swallow Smooth funnel-shaped tapering (green overlay) in the region of the lower esophageal sphincter is accompanied by widening (arrow) of the prestenotic distal esophagus (red overlay). The appearance of the esophagus in achalasia has been referred to as the “bird-beak sign” (illustration). If the contour of the narrowed region is irregular and/or involves more than just the distal esophagus, the alternative diagnosis of esophageal carcinoma (pseudoachalasia) must be considered.

Photomicrograph of a kidney tissue sample (PAS stain, medium magnification) Various elements of the renal tubular and vascular system are seen in different sections. An arteriole is visible on the left (arrow), showing a thick, laminated wall (onion-skin appearance) due to hyperplasia of the smooth muscle tissue (examples of nuclei indicated by yellow overlay) and reduplicated basement membrane material, which stains PAS-positive (magenta-colored laminae; examples indicated by arrowheads). The lumen is severely narrowed as a consequence of these vessel wall changes. These are typical features of hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis.

Photomicrograph of peripheral blood smear Numerous healthy erythrocytes (E) and several fragmented erythrocytes (black dashed circles) with a normal convex side (colored blue) and a fragmented side that is concave or straight (colored red) can be seen. Schistocytes are a diagnostic finding in microangiopathic hemolytic anemias. These cellular fragments can also result from mechanical damage to erythrocytes (e.g., due to prosthetic heart valves).
Photograph of the left thumb A 2-3 cm dark red, round, shiny mass can be seen at the interphalangeal joint. This appearance is characteristic of a pyogenic granuloma.

Ventricular systole extends between the beginning of the R wave and the end of the T wave of the ECG. The phonocardiogram depicts a crescendo-decrescendo (diamond-shaped) late systolic ejection murmur, which is characteristic of an aortic stenosis murmur. The S1 and S2 heart sounds create the high-frequency waves at both ends of the murmur's sound wave.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (May Grunwald Giemsa stain) Multiple erythrocytes are visible, some of which contain small round inclusions (example indicated by yellow overlay). These inclusions consist of denatured hemoglobin and are called Heinz bodies. They are classically associated with G6PD deficiency, but can also be seen in other conditions.

X-ray legs (AP view) of a 2-year-old child with rickets Deficiency in mineralization has resulted in bowing of the legs (genu varum; indicated by dashed lines). Excess nonmineralized osteoid at the metaphyseal zones of provisional calcification has produced widening of the epiphyseal growth plate (examples indicated by green overlay) along with metaphyseal splaying (examples indicated by red lines), cupping (yellow line), and fraying (examples indicated by blue overlay).

X-ray of the right femorotibial joint (AP view) of a 17-year-old male patient A sclerotic outgrowth of the bone (green overlay) surrounded by osteolytic destruction of medullary and cortical bone (red overlay) is visible on the medial metaphysis of the femur. The periosteum over the lesion is noncontinuous (dashed line), indicating rapid growth of the bone lesion. This appearance of a mixed osteosclerotic and osteolytic lesion in the metaphyses of long bones is characteristic of osteosarcoma.
CT head (without contrast; axial plane) Hyperdensity (green overlay) in the basal cisterns indicates the presence of subarachnoid hemorrhage.
White discoloration of the 4th digit of the left hand distal to the proximal interphalangeal joint (ischemic phase). The other fingers are not affected.

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of a newborn with duodenal atresia A nasogastric tube terminates in the gastric antrum. Gaseous distention of the stomach and pyloric channel (green overlay) and dilatation of the duodenal bulb (red overlay) are seen. Bowel distal to the duodenal bulb does not contain gas. This appearance has been termed the “double-bubble sign.” This sign is seen with duodenal atresia and several other congenital and acquired causes of duodenal obstruction.

CT thorax (axial plane) Extensive radiolucent gas (pneumomediastinum; green overlay) is seen throughout the prevascular tissues and adjacent to the trachea and esophagus. Some gas has tracked from the mediastinum into the subcutaneous tissues (subcutaneous emphysema) and is visible within the left breast (examples indicated by red arrowheads). E: esophagus; T: trachea
Slit-lamp photography of a left eye A brown ring is visible in the periphery of the cornea (Kayser-Fleischer ring; green overlay). Kayser-Fleischer rings are typically seen in Wilson disease.
Reactive (normal) fetal nonstress test There is a baseline heart rate of ∼130/min (white dotted line) with four accelerations (numbers) within the recorded window (recording shows 13.5 minutes).

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Sinus rhythm with a heart rate of approx. 77/min (yellow overlay)
– Normal cardiac axis
– Prolonged PR interval (~220 ms)
– ST-segment depression in I, II, and V2 (example indicated by blue overlay in zoom box) to V6
– T-wave flattening in the limb leads and inversion in the precordial leads (example indicated by pink overlay)
– Prominent U waves in the precordial leads (example indicated by green overlay). The P waves in these leads are mostly buried in the U waves (example indicated by red overlay). These findings are characteristic of severe hypokalemia.
Soles of the feet of a child Macular, vesicular lesions on an erythematous base are visible on the toes and the soles of the feet. This rash is typical in hand, foot, and mouth disease.

X-ray chest (PA view) A large pneumothorax (green overlay) has resulted in collapse of the right lung along the mediastinum (edge marked by white dashed line). No bronchovascular markings are seen in the right hemithorax. Additionally, the mediastinum has shifted to the contralateral left side (blue line and arrows), the right hemidiaphragm is depressed (red line and arrow), and the right intercostal spaces are widened (example indicated by white arrows). These findings are characteristic of tension pneumothorax.

Fundus photography of the left eye There are drusen (yellow deposits, area marked by dashed blue circle) and retinal pigment epithelium atrophy (blue overlay). Blue circle: optic disc
M: macula

Photomicrograph of a sputum specimen (Gram stain and safranin counterstain; 100x) Short, gram-negative, rod-shaped bacteria (pink appearance due to the safranin counterstain; examples indicated by green overlay) can be seen. Some of them have formed short chains. Several neutrophil granulocytes are also visible (examples indicated by arrowheads).

X-ray knee (lateral view) of an adolescent with a painful swelling below the knee The fragmented appearance of the tibial tuberosity (fragments indicated by green overlay) with overlying soft tissue swelling (indicated by arrow) and thickening of the patellar tendon (red overlay) supports a diagnosis of apophysitis at the patellar tendon insertion (Osgood-Schlatter disease).

CT head (without IV contrast; axial section; brain window) A crescent-shaped, heterogeneous lesion that crosses cranial sutures is visible on the anterolateral aspect of the right hemisphere. The anterior aspect of the lesion is hypodense (green overlay) indicating a chronic hematoma (serum extrusion and degradation of the clot decreases its radiodensity). The posterior aspect of the lesion is isodense to hyperdense (red overlay), indicating a recent hemorrhage into the existing hematoma (recent blood clots contain concentrated hemoglobin, which is hyperdense). Mass effect is visualized as a shift in the midline (white line) to the left (dashed white line), compression of the left lateral ventricle, and compression of the adjacent cerebral parenchyma, which appears edematous (hyperdense with loss of the sulci and gyri). These features are characteristic of an acute-on-chronic subdural hematoma.

Cranial MRI (T1-weighted; with contrast; axial view) An intensely contrast-enhancing, homogeneous mass (green overlay) is visible in the right cerebellopontine angle. This is the typical appearance of an acoustic neuroma.

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of a premature neonate Multiple loops of gas-filled bowel are seen, some of which are accompanied by linear (example indicated by red arrowhead) and bubbly (examples indicated by green arrowheads) lucencies that indicate intramural gas (pneumatosis intestinalis; areas in green circles). Subtle linear lucencies (examples indicated by black arrowheads) projecting over the right upper quadrant might indicate portal venous gas. There is no pneumoperitoneum. An NG tube (yellow overlay) terminates in the stomach.
Partially confluent, hyperkeratotic papules with a rough surface are seen on the dorsal surface of the hand at the level of the metacarpophalangeal joint

X-ray chest (left: PA view; right: right lateral decubitus view) A solid ovoid mass (red overlay) in the dependent portion of a large right upper lobe cavity (blue outlines) seen on the PA radiograph moves to a dependent position on a subsequent right lateral decubitus radiograph. Saprophytic (noninvasive) aspergillus develops in the form of aspergilloma (also called a fungus ball or mycetoma) in preexisting cavities, such as those resulting from tuberculosis or sarcoidosis. Surrounding air has a crescentic appearance (Monod sign; green overlay). Movement to a dependent location with a change in patient position supports the diagnosis of aspergilloma.

Fundus photography of a right eye Narrow retinal arteries with box-carring of the retinal vessels (black dashed outline) and a pale retina indicating nerve fiber layer edema are visible. The fovea centralis appears red (cherry-red spot; blue overlay) due to the transparency of this part of the retina lacking nerve fibres, causing the well-vascularized choroid to shine through. Additionally, at the temporal superior arcade an embolus is present (arrow-head). This is the classical finding of acute central retinal artery occlusion. Blue circle: Optic disc

An erythematous, scaling rash with irregular borders and erosion of the nipple is visible on the left breast of a female patient. The pathology extends beyond the areola region. These findings are typical of Paget's disease of the breast, a rare type of ductal carcinoma that is often accompanied by nipple discharge. Age-appropriate imaging and biopsy are required for diagnosis.

Photomicrograph of a lymphatic tissue specimen (H&E stain, high magnification) In the center of the image is a typical Reed-Sternberg cell (yellow outline) with two nuclei (blue overlay), each with a prominent cherry-red nucleolus (black arrowheads). This creates the characteristic owl's-eye appearance of Reed-Sternberg cells. Additionally, numerous lymphocytes with basophilic nuclei (arrows) are visible. Reed-Sternberg cells are pathognomonic for Hodgkin lymphoma.

Ventricular systole extends between the beginning of the R wave and the end of the T wave of the ECG. The phonocardiogram depicts a decrescendo diastolic murmur, which is characteristic of an aortic regurgitation murmur. The S1 and S2 heart sounds create the high-frequency waves at both ends of the murmur's sound wave.
Sites of action in the nephron of different classes of diuretics.

Photomicrograph of a breast tissue specimen (H&E stain; low magnification) A hypercellular stroma (example indicated by blue overlay) is visible in the right half of the image. Several clefts or slit-like spaces (examples indicated by black dashed line) lined by darker stained epithelial cells are visible within the stroma, giving it a leaf-like appearance. These are characteristic features of a phyllodes tumor. Normal breast tissue is visible in the left half of the image.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (Wright-Giemsa stain; medium power magnification) There is a mixture of normal erythrocytes, with a uniform, smooth, and rounded surface (blue dashed circles), and erythrocytes, with numerous, evenly spaced, thorny projections (blue circles). These cells are referred to as echinocytes or burr cells. Echinocytes can occur as a result of oxidative or colloid osmotic stress. They may also be an artifact resulting from prolonged storage of blood samples.

X-ray hands (PA view) There is generalized periarticular osteopenia (example indicated by green overlay) and narrowing of several metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joint spaces (example indicated by green line). The marked deformity of the wrists is the result of numerous carpal, base of metacarpal, and radiocarpal erosions (red lines). Erosive scalloping (example indicated by yellow line) also involves the radioulnar joint. The radiographic findings are typical of rheumatoid arthritis with advanced wrist involvement.
Multiple, well-demarcated, partially confluent, erythematous patches located on the posterior thighs. This appearance is typical of mycosis fungoides.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Numerous crescent-shaped erythrocytes, known as “sickle cells,” can be seen (examples indicated by blue overlay; two normal erythrocytes are indicated by arrowheads for comparison). Erythrocytes with a red center and a white contouring ring, known as “target cells” (yellow overlay; cf. illustration) are visible at the bottom of the image. Sickle cells are pathognomonic of sickle-cell disease. Target cells can be found in sickle-cell disease and other hemoglobinopathies.

X-ray foot (AP view) A fracture (green line) of the distal third of the second metatarsal is accompanied by mature callus formation (red overlay). The location and appearance are suggestive of an old stress fracture with nonunion. Radiographs performed at the onset of symptoms of a stress fracture may be normal or may show subtle findings (e.g., inconspicuous fracture line, loss of cortical density, cortical sclerosis or thickening, periosteal elevation). Mature callus is a later finding.

The respiratory amplitude graph (top panel) depicts inspiration as upstrokes and expiration as downstrokes, with the peak-to-peak amplitude of a single waveform representing the tidal volume of a single breath. In Cheyne-Stokes respiration, a crescendo-decrescendo pattern of tidal volumes alternates with periods of apnea.
The lower panel depicts the corresponding oscillation in oxygen saturation.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Rate: ∼70/min
– Rhythm: bigeminy - alternating sinus complexes (examples indicated by green circles) and premature ventricular complexes (PVCs; examples indicated by red lines)
– Axis: normal or slight left axis deviation
– Nonspecific intraventricular conduction delay in the sinus complexes (QRS duration > 0.12s but no clear right or left bundle branch block morphology)
– The PVCs are unifocal (all PVCs in a given lead look the same, indicating they come from the same focus) and most likely arise in the left ventricle (positive in V1, negative in V5/V6)

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Heart rate: ∼ 78/min
– Regular rhythm with no detectable P waves (examples indicated by green circles)
– Normal axis (R > S in I and aVF)
– Widened QRS complexes (∼ 140 ms)
– Tall, peaked T waves (examples indicated by red circles) The combination of tall, peaked T waves, QRS complex widening, and absent P waves suggests moderate to severe hyperkalemia. Note that there is only a weak correlation between serum K+ levels and the severity of ECG changes.

Grouped papulovesicular eruptions with excoriations and surrounding erythema are visible on the lower arms/elbows (A) and knees (B). These findings, together with pruritus, suggest dermatitis herpetiformis.
Frequency-dependent auditory threshold, measured via air and bone conduction In noise-induced hearing loss, hearing is most impaired at frequencies of 4000 Hz in both bone and air conduction.

Photograph of the left arm of a patient There are several large, nodular lesions on the left arm. The largest and most distal lesion is ulcerated. The chain-like pattern of these lesions follows a lymphocutaneous distribution. This is the typical appearance of lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis.

Caudal view (A) Right optic nerve
(B) Optic chiasm
(C) Left optic nerve
(D) Right optic tract
(E) Left optic tract
(F) Right oculomotor nerve
(G) Left oculomotor nerve

(A) Aortic pressure is much greater than left ventricular pressure. Point A occurs right after atrial contraction (causing the first hump on the left atrial pressure curve; atrial kick) and marks the beginning of ventricular systole (isovolumetric contraction). (B) Opening of the aortic valve as the left ventricular pressure exceeds the aortic pressure. (B) to (C) Left ventricular contraction. Left ventricular and aortic pressures rise as the left ventricle ejects blood into the aorta. Pressures within the left ventricle and aorta are almost identical during this period. (C) Peak aortic and left ventricular pressures. After this point, the left ventricular and aortic pressures begin to decrease (reduced ejection phase). The aortic valve is still open during this phase. (D) Closure of the aortic valve as the left ventricular pressure falls below the aortic pressure. This marks the beginning of diastole (isovolumetric relaxation). (E) There is an increase in the aortic pressure (the dicrotic notch) that is caused by the closure of the aortic valve. Left ventricular pressure falls rapidly as the ventricles relax. Point E represents the highest pressure difference between the aorta and the left ventricle (aorta > left ventricle). It is responsible for the peak coronary blood flow at this phase. (F) The aortic pressure decreases gradually during ventricular relaxation. Due to its inherent vascular resistance, aortic pressures remain higher than left ventricular pressures during ventricular diastole.

CT chest (with contrast; coronal plane) of a patient with malignant mesothelioma Nodular pleural thickening (hatched green overlay) encases the left lung and the volume of the left hemithorax (red dashed line) is reduced compared to the right (green dashed line). The findings are compatible with malignant mesothelioma, although confluent pleural metastatic disease can have a similar appearance. Both primary and secondary pleural malignancies can cause pleural nodules, diffuse pleural thickening, and pleural effusion. AO: aorta; LV: left ventricle; PA: pulmonary artery; red overlay: enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes

Each dot on both of these graphs represents a single observation. Left: In general, as the x-values (mean maternal BMI before pregnancy) of the data points increase, the y-values (mean childhood BMI at 4-year follow-up) also increase. This indicates a positive correlation between mean maternal BMI before pregnancy and mean childhood BMI at 4-year follow-up, with a statistically significant value of the correlation coefficient (r=0.45, P<0.01) Right: In this graph, there is no linear relationship between the x-values (mean maternal BMI before pregnancy) and y-values (mean childhood BMI at 4-year follow-up). As such, the correlation coefficient is small and not statistically significant (r=0.03, p=0.23)

X-ray pelvis (AP view) of a 7-year-old child with atraumatic right hip pain and limp The right femoral epiphysis (green overlay) is fragmented (red lines) and smaller than the left epiphysis. There is flattening of its articular surface. Dashed line: normal shape of femoral articular surface; GS: gonad shield
